Follow up from other comment about unit test, I have an idea/suggestion:

How about instead of adding this module here in `examples/platforms/utils` we implement this in OT core itself and add it under `src/core/utils`?

We can treat "power calibration" feature similar to how we handle "otPlatSettings" (`Settings`) and "flash":
- We define a bunch of `otPlatSettings{}` platform APIs.
- Platform can choose to implement `otPlatSettings{}` APIs directly (however they want) 
- Or they can enable `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_PLATFORM_FLASH_API_ENABLE` and then no need to provide `otPlatSettings` APIs and our flash implementation in `src/core/utils` will be used.

Considering similar model for "power calibration":
- We define `otPlatRadio{}` APIs for power calibration in `plratform/radio.h`
  - Platform can choose to implement them directly
  - Or they can choose to use the implementation we provide in OT core.
- We add `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_RADIO_POWER_CALIBRATION_TABLE_ENABLE` feature in OT: 
   - When enabled, we add the implementation of `PowerCalibration`.
   - We can add also a bunch of configs to set the size of table, size of settings data, etc. (for our OT feature).

Some (small) added benefits of moving `PowerCalibration` in the core:
- We can use all the library and helper classes/methods in OT, `Array`, `Clearable`, `VerifyOrExit`, etc  in the code
- Adding unit test for it fits better (as it is a module in OT).

Thoughts?

My thought process on this (and I'm definitely not an expert in all the intricacies of pybind11 ABI compatibility) is that the internals struct in pybind11 is shared between all the modules. Any changes to that definitely requires a lot of careful thought about whether backwards compatibility is being broken. The `local_internals` that I added here should, by design, be segregated to a single module. Whether a module has local internals or not should not impact any other modules, because the only things accessing the local internals is the module that contains them.
If this makes sense to you as well, I'll happily add a comment to the end of this PR explaining that thought process

The content here feels like it's a little more on the side of a design doc or a README for plugin developers - it's not really something users would need to know.  That seems a little at odds with the location of this doc, since currently this page (`docs/timeseries_dashboard.md`) is alongside our user-facing plugin docs that are actually published on tensorflow.org/tensorboard.  (This one would also end up published there, although since it's not in the YAML file it wouldn't be very discoverable.)

Conventionally, the more developer-oriented docs have gone in `plugins/foo/README.md` - sometimes that content is also more user-facing but if there's developer-facing content, that's where we put it, e.g.

- https://cs.opensource.google/tensorflow/tensorboard/+/master:tensorboard/plugins/pr_curve/README.md
- https://cs.opensource.google/tensorflow/tensorboard/+/master:tensorboard/plugins/graph/README.md

So I'd recommend putting this content there, and then either omitting this file, or including only the user-facing aspects there (and in that case also adding it to the YAML file so it's linked from the site nav).

(1) I tried a few things, for the trailing zeroes, but it is not trivial since there are a number of instances where the uncertainty is quoted in the paper with more than two significant figures (most often in the cases of partially calculated values), or e.g. H-4 which has a value of 4.026430+/-110. I have added a note stating that the trailing zeroes do not describe the known uncertainty.

(2) There were already '#'s in the table so i decided it was best to continue the schema. I have added a note to the docstring detailing that these values are not solely experimentally derived.

(3) For the isotopic averages, I noticed an interesting feature, that the uncertainty of the natural abundance mass does not match the uncertainties of the natural abundances. Take for example Li, natural abundance mass is 6.941(2) - an uncertainty of 0.029%, however the abundances are Li-6: 7.59(4), and Li-7: 92.41(4) (total abundance is therefore 100.0(5.7) - e.g. an uncertainty of .57%). Therefore, if i propagate the quoted uncertainties in the abundances, there are quite a few instances where the values disagree (Li being the first). I can ignore the uncertainty propagation from the abundances, or include them and will end up changing quite a few of the values, with the effect of probably increasing the uncertainty of many of the values. If that's the case, the credential needs it to decide where to send the token request.

Using this information in the credential is awkward (though still possible) because the client's authority is set at construction. The client's token cache shares this behavior though, so I'm thinking we'll need to use (and document) a workaround initially: a user can set `AZURE_AUTHORITY_HOST` or pass `authority=KnownAuthorities...` to match the cloud set in VS Code.

The reason is that the specification (RFC 2616 cited above or RFC7230 which appears more recent) states that in HTTP messages, lines are folded specifically by CRLF (`
`) and not by LF (`
`). Not following this had lead to an issue where a header field value containing a LF (`
`) was accidentally interpreted as a multiline field value. Without leading at least one space or tab char in the following line, however, the header ended up being interpreted as malformed and the lines were simply discarded when parsed out.

This is rather pathological and use of LF within a field value feels like a bad idea, but it doesn't appear to violate the specification so some HTTP messages with that structure exist.

Line 84 (above): `addition_pool_args['retries'] = configuration.retries`

This configures the urllib3 connection pool to retry, which is not desirable -- firstly, the retrying is already handled by tenacity at the `api_client` level, and secondly, this means that urllib will wrap the failure in a `MaxRetryError` which will prevent the higher level retry logic from correctly detecting the type of exception in its decision whether to retry or not.

```python
    def __getattr__(self, name: str) -> Any:
        try:
            return self.__dict__["_updated_attrs"][name]
        except KeyError:
            pass

        try:
            value = self.__dict__["_attrs"][name]

            # If the value is a list, we copy it in the _updated_attrs dict
            # because we are not able to detect changes made on the object
            # (append, insert, pop, ...). Without forcing the attr
            # creation __setattr__ is never called, the list never ends up
            # in the _updated_attrs dict, and the update() and save()
            # method never push the new data to the server.
            # See https://github.com/python-gitlab/python-gitlab/issues/306
            #
            # note: _parent_attrs will only store simple values (int) so we
            # don't make this check in the next except block.
            if isinstance(value, list):
                self.__dict__["_updated_attrs"][name] = value[:]
                return self.__dict__["_updated_attrs"][name]

            return value
        except KeyError:
            pass

        try:
            return self.__dict__["_parent_attrs"][name]
        except KeyError as exc:
            message = (
                f"{type(self).__name__!r} object has no attribute {name!r}"
            )
            if self._created_from_list:
                message = (
                    f"{message}\n\n"
                    + textwrap.fill(
                        f"{self.__class__!r} was created via a list() call and "
                        f"only a subset of the data may be present. To ensure "
                        f"all data is present get the object using a "
                        f"get(object.id) call. For more details, see:"
                    )
                    + f"\n\n{_URL_ATTRIBUTE_ERROR}"
                )
            raise AttributeError(message) from exc
```

Async Cluster implementation is not using the connection pool from asyncio client implementation, which uses locks for providing multi-process & multi-threading support. Async Cluster doesn't support multi-process yet & multi-threading isn't supported with asyncio anyway: https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-dev.html#asyncio-multithreading.

It also uses deques instead of lists/sets, so adding/removing connections from the pool is already atomic. These differences improved speed by 9-10% AFAIR. The deque change should be adopted for standalone async clients as well.

Also, we didn't talk about `RootRule`s last week. A `RootRule` is something that can act at the "root" of the rule graph, rather than needing to be computed via some other rule. Generally, we use it to set up the "instrinsics" (aka builtins) of the V2 engine, such as some of our filesystem abstractions like `Digest`:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/73c6e7b253ed2a4fc73cafc92dda4e7778be1731/src/python/pants/engine/fs.py#L277-L286 

In tests, we add use `RootRule` because we're not setting up the full graph used in production. Normally, `pants/backend/python/lint/python_lint_target.py` would provide a rule to "reach" `BanditTarget`, but `python_lint_target.py` is not registered in the test's rule graph, so we must register `BanditTarget` as a root.

Here, we can remove most these `RootRules` because using `OptionsBootstrapper` will now provide a way to "reach" these subsystems like `PythonSetup`.

The issue here is that even though python allows mixing types in a list, the type system rightfully tells you some thing is wrong if you append a different type to a list declared of a certain type.

```python
# type_test.py
import typing as t
import pydash as _


my_list: t.List[int] = [1]

my_list.append("a")
```
```bash
$ mypy type_test.py
type_test.py:7:16: error: Argument 1 to "append" of "list" has incompatible type "str"; expected "int"  [arg-type]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```

But for this function I was thinking more flexibility would be nice. The way I typed it, you can pass a list of integers and a string element and the result will be a list of `Union[int, str]` without any error like `append` would report. This means we have to cast the array to bypass the error appending another type would report.

Another way to right this function could be:
```python
    new_array = t.cast(t.List[t.Union[T, T2]], array)

    for item in items:

    return new_array
```

Also you have to be careful in optimizing dicts away in Python. They are usually really fast and a dict access might be faster than all the comparisons that you are doing here, which sounds maybe counter intuitive, but dict access is really really fast, because it's what Python uses for everything. Comparisons (and math in general) are really slow, because of all the type checking. So intution can often be wrong for such things (also you are doing a ton of dict accesses anyway. `field_tree_instance.name.`, `cls.inference_state.builtins_module` and `.name is also one. I switched to `TRIM_HORIZON` here because it's more reliable. My understanding of the `serverless` tests is that infra gets stood up in a fairy async way. Using `LATEST`, if we send records to Kinesis before the event source mapping is declared and becomes fully active then those records will never be processed. `TRIM_HORIZON` ensures that all records will be processed regardless of the order in which those operations occur.
I agree that it would be great to test both configurations. Ideally we could do this using the `get_event_source_mapping()` API by polling the state of the mapping and waiting until it becomes `ACTIVE` before we send records. Unfortunately our implementation of this API is not yet robust enough to do this. It always reports a mapping as ACTIVE even if the associated EventSourceListener instance hasn't finished coming up. In order to make this work we will need to refactor the EventSourceListener class to make use of some kind of EventSourceListenerManager singleton that can accurately understand and manage the state of the various event source mappings (or at least that's the right way to do it IMO).

In the Glyphs3's `kerningRTL` dictionary, if the _first_ key is a group it's prefixed `@MMK_R_`, whereas the second key in the (nested) dict, is prefixed `@MMK_L_` if a group. Hence, in RTL kerning, the (logical) first glyph is the one placed on the right-hand side, the second glyph is the one on the left-hand side. The opposite is true for LTR kerning, where first is left and second is right. All good so far.

Now, the meaning of "left" and "right" in the `leftKerningGroup` and `rightKerningGroup` glyph properties is _not_ "the group this glyph belong when it occurs in the left|right position", but rather the _side_ or _direction_ towards which this glyph is kerned against: so, for RTL kerning (like your `leftAlef` above), "left" means the group pertains to the left (in visual sense) side of the alef glyph, when this is placed in the first (logical) position next to second glyph, whose right (visual) side is the one that participate in the kerning...

```
                 content_type = 'text/plain; charset=utf-8'
             else:
                 mimetype = parse_mimetype(content_type)
                 readl_encoding = mimetype.parameters.get('charset', 'utf-8')
         else:
            if content_type is None:
                content_type = 'text/plain; charset=%s' % encoding
            real_encoding = encoding

 super().__init__(
                value.encode(encoding),
                encoding=real_encoding,
                content_type=content_type,
                *args,
                **kwargs)
```

I think this is fine. `externs::store_set` in Rust basically marshals a bunch of Rust values into a C-style array of Python `Handle`s and then invokes this `extern_store_set` function with `vals_ptr` and `vals_len` set correctly. `extern_store_set` then uses the `_ffi` library tools to unmarshal that array of `Handle`s into Python objects, and calls the object constructor with them in Python. The Rust FFI code shouldn't care whether the Python object constructor this function calls is an `OrderedSet` or a `FrozenOrderedSet`. As far as Rust is concerned the output of this function is an opaque Python `Value` no matter what.

This isn't an error.  All you want to be true is that `bhh` is the hash of an ancestor of the current block (identified by `cur_block_hash`).  `bhh` can live in a different fork column, which is what `fork_id` identifies.

What you want to do is first calculate the block height of `bhh` (call it `bhh_block_height`), and then call `self.storage.fork_table.walk_back(&cur_block_hash, bhh_block_height)` to get the ancestor block hash.  If that block hash is _not_ `bhh`, then `bhh` is not an ancestor of `cur_block_hash` and the method should fail.

> isort and black can and will attempt to modify the same files, for example.

Yes, this is why we use an aggregator for each language. Python has an aggregator `python_fmt_target.py` that will run all the relevant formatters over Python targets _sequentially_ and merge results so that there is no conflict.

So long as the aggregators are implemented correctly, i.e. each language is distinct and does not touch the other, like the Shell formatters don't touch Python files and vice versa, then there will "in practicality" be no merge conflict. If there is a merge conflict, then we want the engine to fail so that we are aware of this situation.

The Ethernet 2 frame consists of 14 bytes header, up to 1500 bytes data and 4 bytes CRC, totalling 1518 bytes.

We should change the default value back to 1514.

```
@pytest.mark.parametrize("auto_request_id,other_request_id", [(True, None), (True, "junk"), (False, None), (False, "junk")])
 def test_request_id_policy_fix_on_demand_id(auto_request_id):
     """Test policy with no other policy and happy path"""
     test_request_id = 'test_request_id'
     request_id_policy = RequestIdPolicy()
     request_id_policy = RequestIdPolicy(auto_request_id=auto_request_id)
     if other_request_id:
         request_id_policy.set_request_id(other_request_id)
     request = HttpRequest('GET', 'http://127.0.0.1/')
     pipeline_request = PipelineRequest(request, PipelineContext(None))
     pipeline_request.context.options['request_id'] = test_request_id
     request_id_policy.on_request(pipeline_request)
     assert request.headers["x-ms-client-request-id"] == test_request_id
```

```transition_matrix = {
            NotificationStatus.DRAFT: [NotificationStatus.READY],
            NotificationStatus.READY: [NotificationStatus.PROCESSING],
            NotificationStatus.PROCESSING: [NotificationStatus.COMPLETED, NotificationStatus.FAILED],
            NotificationStatus.COMPLETED: [NotificationStatus.ARCHIVED],
            NotificationStatus.FAILED: [NotificationStatus.ARCHIVED],
            NotificationStatus.ARCHIVED: []
        }
```

```
    normal lifecycle:

    - DRAFT, when a message is first saved to the backend and the status is still in draft.
        next states: READY
    - READY: when the message is ready for processing, it is complete and is to be processed
        next states: PROCESSING
    - PROCESSING: when the message is in the process of being send out
        next states: COMPLETED, FAILED
    - COMPLETED: when the message is successfully sent out
        next states: ARCHIVED
    - FAILED: when the message is not successfully sent out
        next states: ARCHIVED
    - ARCHIVED: when the message is archives and waiting for its deletion against a certain retention policy
        next states: None
```

```python
from ray.util.sgd.torch.helpers import default_dataloaders
 
def data_creator(...):
    train_dataset = LinearDataset(2, 5, size=config.get("data_size", 1000))
```

@epicfaace In the previous version of the test, It will just cycle through the list of large Docker images. `docker_image_manager.py` uses an LRU cache to store the Docker images. With an LRU cache, it will simply kick out the images earlier in the list, dealing with the same sequence of image sizes over and over again. Now if we start picking images at random, the LRU cache will have to deal with varying sizes of Docker images coming in, which better mimics real-world behavior.

To your second point, we select an image from a list of 20 images uniformly at random 2000 times. Each image in the list should be used multiple times. If a specific image is problematic, it will be caught by this stress test and fail. Also, our main concern and the point of the stress test is to see if our system can create a bunch of bundles with large Docker images and not if a certain image causes failures or not.

This function is not aligning access, it is loading an array of little-endian order bytes into a `uint64_t`. Which is then returned to the caller. This tells Ray Data to compute the entire preprocessed and pin it in Ray object store memory. As a result, when iterating over the dataset repeatedly, the preprocessing operations do not need to be re-run.

```suggestion
However, if the preprocessed data is too large to fit into Ray object store memory, this will greatly decrease performance as data needs to be spilled to and read back from disk.
```

In a wider review context, I would like to propose to access the status bits returned by command "B" in a more structured way. In this mode, your code is probably slower, but much more readable, in my opinion.
My proposal is to define a static method which decodes the bytes returned by "B" command through the use of ctypes and bitfield (see attached examples [test.zip](https://github.com/pymeasure/pymeasure/files/6411976/test.zip))

Let's call the static method `get_status(status, field=None)` with the following behaviour:
- `get_status(status, None)` return all the fields
- `get_status(status, "auto_zero")` return "auto_zero" field

Advantages:
- code is more readable
- `__str__` call can replace `status_readable` utility
- `status get_process` is defined as follow:` get_process=(lambda x: self.get_status(x.encode(encoding="ASCII")))`
- `auto_zero` can be rewritten as follow:
```
auto_zero = Instrument.control(
        "B",
        "Z%d",
        """ Return autozero settings on the HP3478, this property can be set """,
        get_process=(lambda x: self.get_status(x.encode(encoding="ASCII"), "auto_zero")),
        validator=strict_discrete_set,
        values=(0,1),
        )
```
- similar approach also for other property and and methods in the class, when possible
- Using `map_value` and dictionaries allow to use symbolic names as well (see `https://pymeasure.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dev/adding_instruments.html`), so probably your dictionaries `MODES`, `RANGES`, etc. can be reused.
- Align code to class `Instrument` style and make it probably more homogenous to the rest of instruments.

* IIUC `\mathrm` can be used for making mathematical operators like `\sin`, `\cos`, etc, but I think that `\DeclareMathOperator{\ord}{ord}` is the preference?
* FYI: for variables that have more than one letter you can use [`\mathit{}`](https://tex.stackexchange.com/questions/218653/making-math-behaving-like-mathit) which _mostly_ fixes spacing? Sorry for maybe missing the essence of your question in my earlier reply! The point is that the current behavior around `NaN`s for the `LabelEncoder` is unusable. There is no guarantee in the ONNX standard about preserving the bit-patterns of `NaN`s in any operation or even directly in the inputs. Matching against a particular pattern, as is currently suggested by the documentation of the `LabelEncoder`, leads to an undefined hit-and-miss behavior. It might work but is really neither reliable nor convenient to work with. I don't see the point in leaving it as is, to be honest.

So the question becomes if it should be removed, or if the specification should be updated to something workable. If it were to be removed one would indeed have to work around with `IsNaN` and `Where`. Not the nicest, but workable

The alternative is the suggested "upgrade" where all `NaN` values are treated as equal. This does not incur a high cost on the implementation side where the equality and hash operation would simply gain a special case for `NaN`. 

The use case we are seeing is that sometimes we get floating point inputs to our models which are essentially categorical encodings (including `NaN`s). We use label encoders to map those floating point categories to integers or strings but don't want to necessarily map `NaN`s to the "default/unseen" case.

I've been thinking more on  this, and I understand why these APIs in `radio.h` are needed/added. However, I'm not sure if `radio.h` is the best place for them.

Here are my thoughts:

- We want to use OT's "multiple instance" model, where each `otInstance` corresponds to a different PAN.
- Each `ot::Instance` will assume that it has an `ot::Radio` and can control it using the `otPlatRadio` APIs.
- We want the platform layer to handle multi-PAN logic.
- This is mainly for the case where there is one radio and we want to share it between PANs. In this case, the platform layer needs to make it look like there are multiple radios, each controlled by a different set of `otPlatRadio()` APIs called with a different `otInstance *` input.
- I think the `ot::Radio` module should not be aware of the multi-PAN feature. Therefore, I'm not sure about adding these APIs to `radio.h` and related modules.
- One solution would be to define a new `openthread/platform/multipan.h` platform header and define these APIs in that header. 
    - This would give us a dedicated place to define APIs for multi-PAN support, and it would also allow us to add more APIs in the future if needed (e.g., APIs for arbitrating conflicting requests).

What do you think?

Nitpicking your words to make sure things are _crystal clear_:
You can specify an entry point in 3 ways when building a pex:
1. -c <named console script>: these are entrypoint functions granted names in setup.py
2. -c <named script>: these are actual scripts granted names in setup.py
3. -e <entrypoint>: this is an arbitrary entrypoint function; ie: `myapp.submodule:nullary_function_name`

When building a pex, method 3 is the only way to have a specific arbitrary function invoked. Method 1 only allows pre-named functions ("console scripts") to be invoked.

The way I see this is we want to use "multiple instance" feature to realize multi-PAN.  Each `ot::Instance` (whether on host or on RCP) runs on its own and gets to call all platform APIs, including `otPlatRadio()`, using its `otInstance *`. Each `ot::Instance` assumes that it is sole controller of all its platform/radio.

To support multiple-PAN when there is really a single radio which we want to share, each PAN is running its own `otInstance` and is not aware of existence of others. We would need a module/process that arbitrate the action of single radio (make it look like and behave as if it is multiple radios). This module is aware of all `otInstance` objects (each corresponding to a PAN) and can make decisions based on the plat API commands issued to radio by each `otInstance` (e.g., which instance is allowed to actively using/controlling radio at a time). 
This module itself would not be tied to any particular `ot::Instance` and can use the `otPlatMultipan{}()` APIs to control the radio (which are special platform APIs in the sense that they are also not tied to any `otInstance *`).

Does this make make sense?

auto is good for readability for interesting derived types like iterators and stuff where the actual type of the object is "far away", or types may change due to overloading.
```
void do_something(std::map<std::string, size_t>& idx) {
   ...
    auto iter = idx.find(...)
}
```
where the alternative is kind of inconvenient
```
void do_something(std::map<std::string, size_t>& idx) {
    ...
    std::map<std::string, size_t>::iterator iter = idx.find(...) 
}
```
And also requires a lot of code changes if the argument types change in anyway.
```
void do_something(const std::map<std::string, size_t>& idx) {
    ...
    std::map<std::string, size_t>::iterator iter = idx.find(...)  // compile error. should be const_iterator
}
```

Also useful for things like range based for loops over complicated types (really just as above the "correct" type for a range based for loop is std::map<std::string, size_t>::value_type, that gets a little annoying.

But I will prefer that auto is not used for simple types, or potentially platform dependent types. 
```
uint64_t i = 0; // good. I can tell immediately what width this integer is and if it is signed.
auto i = 0; // bad. Hard to tell immediately.
float j = 0.0; //good. I know that this is a 32-bit float
auto j = 0.0; //bad. How wide is j?
```
This is crucial. For instance when we were porting from Linux/Mac to Windows, it was far easier to search for the "long" keyword to replace all uses of "long" with int64_t or "long long" (long is 32-bit on Windows, 64-bit elsewhere). Whereas if we had used "auto" this would have been significantly harder to fix.

```
az config set core.output=table
```

> Property to be set. Note that SECTION/ is optional while referring to properties in the core section, i.e., using either core/project or project is a valid way of setting a project. That's whats written in [The Elsevier DTD 5 Family of XML DTDs](https://www.elsevier.com/__data/assets/pdf_file/0010/851626/tagbytag5-v1.9.7.pdf):
```
• crown is used when the author claims Crown copyright. 
• free-of-copyright: this value is used when the item has no copyright, for example in case of an index. 
• full-transfer: this value is used when a full transfer to one of the publisher’s
companies has been received.
• joint is used when a full transfer has been received for an article in a journal whose
copyright is owned jointly by one of the publisher’s companies and a society. 
• limited-transfer is used when the author has granted only limited rights; special
care must be taken for its production.
• no-transfer is used when there is an unresolvable copyright problem and the article may not be published (in principle, documents with this copyright status cannot
occur). This should not be confused with copyright status 001, when copyright has
not yet been transferred. Within ce:figure, ce:textbox or ce:e-component it
is used to signal that the object may not appear online.
• other is used when copyright owner is different from the journal’s copyright owner,
e.g. the authors or their employing institutions. This copyright type is also used in
the ce:copyright within a ce:figure, ce:textbox or ce:e-component. 
• society is used when a full transfer has been received for an article in a journal
whose copyright is owned by a society. 
• unknown: this value is used when the article may be published but the actual status
is unknown. This is, for instance, the case when the copyright transfer form has not
yet been received from the author. 
• us-gov is used when the author is a US government employee and will not transfer
copyright. 
```

The batch size used on each horovod process and so every GPU on every machine is the batch size you specified like normally with `DeepSpeech.py --train_batch_size` and is only used on creating dataset

    train_set = create_dataset(FLAGS.train_files.split(','),

https://github.com/tud-zih-tools/DeepSpeech/blob/329bf876069720cf05b4e4700e6d0dde104b6bac/training/deepspeech_training/train.py#L423  (Is it possible to link the code here directly?)

So, your effective batch size for training on which the Optimizer is applyed is `FLAGS.train_batch_size * NUM_PROC` where `NUM_PROC` is the number of used horovod processes which is equally to the number gpus on your whole setup. In Horovod terminus, equally to HPC MPI terminus (horovod.size())

To prevent network convergence problems because of this bigger effective batch size we scale the learning rate as recommented by the horovod devs
with
`FLAGS.learning_rate * NUM_PROC`
https://github.com/tud-zih-tools/DeepSpeech/blob/329bf876069720cf05b4e4700e6d0dde104b6bac/training/deepspeech_training/train.py#L487



In theory horovod has no problem if you apply different batch sizes to each gpus. In practice you want to make sure every process finishes with its batch at about the same time (load balance). If one process is much late horovod error handling will take action.

1. Thread devices generally will not implement the commissioner role. It would be nice not to have to reserve a state changed flag for a role that most devices will not implement.

> and have things like enum34 fall away

That would be more of a "forced" change, to satisfy the specs that the user is requesting in the current operation.

Actually, there are two cases here.  Case number one is when enum34 is the traditional conda package tied to a specific python version.  Case number two is when enum34 is a noarch python package and is "universal," in that it's designed to work with `python =2.7|>=3.3`.

The first case would "force" an enum34 removal when switching the environment from python 2.7 to python 3.6, since there would be no enum34 package for python 3.6.  The second case could feasibly leave the enum34 package in the environment, even though it wouldn't be used or required by any package in the environment after the change.  The solver behavior from 4.4 to 4.6 was designed to make the first case happen automatically, forcing the removal of enum34 if that is what was required to satisfy the specs the user is requesting in the current operation.  The `--prune` flag in 4.4. to 4.6 was designed to address the second case, where the enum34 package would hang around sort of just through "hysteresis," but then provide a way to actually clean up superfluous packages that are no longer a requirement of the full history of user-requested specs.

@mvdbeek : all done. I've converted `fastqReader` and `fastqWriter` to context managers. These are used in all `sequence/scripts` - so that led to more edits. It is my understanding that these classes are *not* used outside this suite. If they are (i.e., if we have code accessing these 2 classes directly), this is a breaking change. Both classes opened the file in their constructors; now this happens in the `__enter__` method: so the file is opened only when the `with` statement is evaluated. But again, as far as I know, the reader/writer are *not* used by outside code.

Implementation detail: I moved some local variables outside the `with` statement if they were accessed *after* that statement, like so:
```
i = None  # in the original code, this was assigned after the reader
with reader:
  i += 1
print(i)
```
I know `with` doesn't define its own scope, but in the unlikely event that (a) an exception is raised and (b) the `__exit__` method has been updated to handle that exception, the `print(i)` line would raise an error. Unlikely scenario, but that's keeping it safe.

Also, I did not convert other readers/writers (like the ones in `fasta.py`) because there are no tests for those.. Also, there are at least a few other places in the code where file handles may remain open. But that would be a different PR.

The short answer is that 'yes', an operator with 3 inputs might make use of 3 streams in Backward(), so I did not want to propose an environment variable name like MXNET_GPU_WORKER_USE_DUAL_STREAM=0/1 that might soon become obsolete.  On the other hand, Convolution only needs 2 streams, and I did not want to burden this enhancement with more complexity than is needed at this time.  I propose that when we have a use-case for 3 or more streams, then we can expand the implementation and employ the use-case in our testing of it.

At the end of every kernel execution, there is a fall-off in GPU utilization leading up to the completion of the last grid block.  When two streams are being used, these utilization gaps can be filled by work from the second stream.  I would guess that having 3 streams would not enhance this effect.  On the other hand, let's say you had 3 small independent kernels that each would occupy a third of the GPU.  You could see how having 3 streams would be a win in this case over 2 streams.

So it's good that you ask, how might we expand this to 3 or more streams?  The MXNET_GPU_WORKER_NSTREAMS environment variable would remain unchanged, though the documentation would indicate that the framework supports a value greater than 2.  Legacy env-var uses would be preserved so I think this could happen as part of a minor release.  At the RunContext level, a GPUAuxStream* would be replaced by a std::vector<GPUAuxStream*>.  The RunContext method get_gpu_aux_stream() might then be changed to RunContex::get_gpu_aux_stream(int aux_stream_id = 0), which would not break operator code that started using the simpler aux_stream API proposed by this PR.

```
  2           0 LOAD_FAST                0 (data)
```

Regarding the cycle/state relationship, it depends on the encoding. Right now, the CoreIR encoder detects this automatically by default. So our "abstracted clock" encoding means that every state transition is a full clock cycle. This happens iff there's a single clock and no negedge behavior. Otherwise, each state transition is a "step" and the clock toggling has to be done manually: so normally you'd have a fastest clock that toggles every step and then possibly slower clocks on a different period. The CoreIR encoder also takes a boolean that can force it to abstract the clock (all clocks become synchronized and neg-edge behavior is dropped): https://github.com/upscale-project/pono/blob/157c4c83728b1d6926a5376f0a7a6b04ddf69c4b/frontends/coreir_encoder.h#L15. I haven't tested that feature much though, so let me know if you run into issues.

Right, so properties in Pono are restricted to being over state variables. I know it's somewhat annoying because CoSA was more relaxed about it, but it's much more predictable in terms of behavior. The simplest way to get around that is just to create a property signal, e.g. a state variable `prop` that keeps track of whether the property is satisfied (this extends the trace by 1).

For example, say you want to check if `x.next == x`. Then you just create a variable `xnext_prop` that starts as `True` and then constrain it like: `rts.constrain_trans(solver.make_term(Iff, rts.next(xnext_prop), solver.make_term(Equal, xnext, x)`. And now your property is just `xnext_prop`. I'm planning to handle that automatically in `Property` for you soon (we already handle it automatically in the binary). I can make a PR for that today. I'm not sure I'll stick with the implementation, but the interface should stay the same at least.

This refactoring *was* needed in order to work with the tests in isolation. Those implicit, unintentional tests were a PITA to isolate and change, which limits their utility. Also, the correctness checks themselves are an example of the building complexity - they are sometimes slightly different for different models, e.g. checking that the memory efficient Densenet implementation showed the same output as the vanilla version. To your other points:

* I'm not married to the test input caching, and if there's a demonstrable issue that I'm not aware of (as opposed to merely a prospective one), I'm happy to change it.
* The introspection was already happening. I only included it so we wouldn't lose the sense of security people seem to get from the implicit/unintentional tests being generated over the factory methods.
* If by "two separate pathways" you mean the difference between classification and other model types, that's true. My brief only covered Classification models. I think the other model types should be broken out into their own files (as their testing needs will likely differ), and updated to dump the opaque generated tests.
* Yes, there is more code, but it's also modularized in a way that explicitly maps the tests to the code under test, removes conditionals in favor of OO, and makes it trivially obvious to find where to make a change or set a breakpoint for a particular model with a text search. "Less code" is not, in every situation, the best variable to optimize for.

Your last point - about test being enforced by process - is orthogonal to the point I made above. If someone adds a model, never looks at the (implicit, unintentional) tests, and the automated tests all show green, the likelihood is against either the dev or a reviewer going deeper. The generated tests leave that door open. The new tests do nothing if the dev codes their tests first, and gives a nudge if they don't.

in general there're two kinds of link level errors:

- 1. errors coming from service which must be via a link detach frame
- 2. errors coming from the client, might be wrong data input, link already closed

to handle errors coming from service, I think LinkDetach is enough (same to Session/Connection as END/CLOSE frame carries the error info).

to handle client side errors, we probably could have this generic "LinkError" as our fallback exception for non-identifies issues and make our error hierarchy extensible.

The advantage of having these foreign assets as standalone objects in repositories instead of inside jobs is that it would enable users to define them without defining jobs that consume them.

Example scenario: there's an analytics team that uses dbt + Airflow to create a bunch of tables in a database. There's also an ML team that wants to use Dagster to build ML models etc. on top of those tables.  The ML team writes a dbt utility that consumes the dbt project and creates a foreign asset for each model inside of it.  Now metadata for all those tables is available in Dagit, means that the ML users can find out info about all the assets they might care about in one place.

The way how Windows parses command-line arguments can be found at [Parsing C++ command-Line arguments](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/cpp/main-function-command-line-args?view=vs-2019#parsing-c-command-line-arguments):

> Microsoft C/C++ startup code uses the following rules when interpreting arguments given on the operating system command line:
> 
> - Arguments are delimited by white space, which is either a space or a tab.
> 
> - The caret character (^) is not recognized as an escape character or delimiter. The character is handled completely by the command-line parser in the operating system before being passed to the `argv` array in the program.
> 
> - A string surrounded by double quotation marks ("*string*") is interpreted as a single argument, regardless of white space contained within. A quoted string can be embedded in an argument.
> 
> - A double quotation mark preceded by a backslash (\\") is interpreted as a literal double quotation mark character (").
> 
> - Backslashes are interpreted literally, unless they immediately precede a double quotation mark.
> 
> - If an even number of backslashes is followed by a double quotation mark, one backslash is placed in the `argv` array for every pair of backslashes, and the double quotation mark is interpreted as a string delimiter.
> 
> - If an odd number of backslashes is followed by a double quotation mark, one backslash is placed in the `argv` array for every pair of backslashes, and the double quotation mark is "escaped" by the remaining backslash, causing a literal double quotation mark (") to be placed in `argv`.

This should work fine for BERT, but won't for other models like XLM that name their modules differently. In general I think it'll be brittle to rely on guessing the internal module structure of transformer models.

But I can think of alternative which - although it's a little less friendly from a user perspective - still provides this useful functionality:
- Change the type of `reinit_layers` to `Optional[Union[int, List[int], List[str]]]`.
- When `reinit_layers` is `List[str]`, we interpret it as a list of regular expressions and we reinitialize all modules that match any of the regular expressions in the list.
- Otherwise when `reinit_layers` is `int` or `List[int]` we try the current approach, but if the given `transformer_model` does not have this module structure we throw a `ConfigurationError` and suggest that the user use the `List[str]` form of this parameter instead.

```python
def _choose_param_value(main_param_name: str, params: Dict[str, Any], default_value: Any):
    # avoid side effects on passed-in parameters
    params = deepcopy(params)

    # find a value, and remove other aliases with .pop()
    found_value = None
    for param in _ConfigAliases.get(main_param_name):
        val = params.pop(param)
        if found_value is None and val is not None:
            found_value = val

    if found_value is not None:
        params[main_param_name] = found_value
    else:
        params[main_param_name] = default_value

    return params
```

An interesting case to consider: if the user sets `HOROVOD_GPU_OPERATIONS=NCCL`, then they get `HOROVOD_GPU_ALLTOALL=NCCL` for free.  But if their NCCL version < 2.7, this creates a runtime error.

Alternatively, we could fall back to using MPI All-to-All instead of raising the error, but then users might get poor performance without knowing why (particularly if they specified `HOROVOD_GPU_ALLTOALL=NCCL` explicitly).

I think that overall the way you have it implemented is the best solution, since there is a workaround for users who cannot upgrade NCCL for whatever reason: instead of using `HOROVOD_GPU_OPERATIONS`, set `HOROVOD_GPU_ALLREDUCE` and omit `HOROVOD_GPU_ALLTOALL`.

This is for the case where we have more than one row partition, and more than one column partition. If we just do self.apply_full_axis, we get N partitions (where N was the number of column partitions we had) partitions with all of the rows, so sorting can fail on a KeyError if not every partition has the sort columns. To avoid this, if we have more than one column partition, we combine all of them so every partition has all of the columns and some of the rows, and then do a full axis sort on the other axis (so we get one big partition with all of the data).

```python
from modin.core.storage_formats.pandas.utils import split_result_of_axis_func_pandas
import pandas

df = pandas.DataFrame({"a": range(34)})
```

The potential issue here is that this PR introduces a distinction between:
- "We no longer need to request any more runs for this backfill"
- "We no longer need to process this backfill within backfill iterations"

I.e., with this PR, we want to keep loading asset backfills after we're done requesting runs, in order to update the `AssetBackfillData` object. This object already needs to keep track of the final status (materialized/failed) of some of the asset partitions in the backfill, so if we can make `AssetBackfillData` keep track of _all_ of them, it makes it simpler and cheaper to query for info about the backfill's progress for the UI.

This means that we need some status that can allow us to distinguish between:
- Backfills where all the requested runs have completed, and thus we should never even load them in the backfill daemon
- Backfills that we want to load in the backfill daemon, either to request new runs for or just to update the data we're tracking for them

A more orthogonal, future looking thing: a target bundle size that might be better than the user-provided `batch_size` is probably something like the following formula:
```python
target_size = max(
    min((prefetch_batches + 1) * batch_size_in_bytes, ctx.target_min_block_size),
    ctx.target_max_block_size,
)
```
I.e. where we bundle up to **at least** the `ctx.target_min_block_size` (default is 1 MiB) since that's what we consider to be the smallest "reasonable" block to make the task overhead worth it; if the number of desired concurrent batches is larger than this (e.g. batch size is larger than this and/or aggressive prefetching is specified), then we use that as a bundling target. And all of this is capped by `ctx.target_max_block_size`.

We'd probably still have the max actor pool size serve as a cap on the number of block bundles as well, but I'd imagine that the initial actor pool size (i.e. actors started at the beginning of execution) and the scale-up rate would be influenced by the number of block bundles.

We should experiment with a few of these hints/policies in the new execution model.

The new code is definitely better than using an f-string, but I believe it's actually fine using `/` in strings for pathlib (it was a problem back in the `os.path` days). The docs do it in many examples, and it is automatically converted to the system separator:
```
>>> str(pathlib.PureWindowsPath("a/b"))
'a\\b'
>>> str(pathlib.PurePosixPath("a/b"))
'a/b'
```

Even when constructing it programmatically, it uses `/` in the `repr` version as a universal representation:
```
>>> p = pathlib.PureWindowsPath("a") / "b"
>>> p
PureWindowsPath('a/b')
>>> str(p)
'a\\b'
```

This method has a concrete use case and it is to define software-defined assets for the dbt sources.

Like you said, the point of dbt sources is to define the source data for the dbt models. In dbt, this defines the upstream dependencies (i.e. we map the `(source_name, table_name)` to an `AssetKey`) of the dbt models that are external to the system.

So, the dependencies are already defined when the dbt project is read by the `dagster-dbt` integration. However, to materialize these dbt sources in Dagster, the user needs to define the logic associated with these upstream `AssetKey`'s.

This function, `get_asset_keys_for_dbt_source`, gives you the `AssetKey`'s that you need to define the software-defined assets for the dbt sources.

The problem with testing against a "real thing" is that it depends on the configuration of that real thing, and requires quite a bit of complexity to establish the conditions for the test -- in this case, creating and claiming a task, creating an artifact on that task, and uploading that artifact.  The result is that the condition-establishment becomes more brittle than the test itself.  In this case, it would depend on configuration of the object service's backends, for example, as well as taskQueues.  It also eliminates the possibility of lots of conditions.  In the example of upload or download support, for example, there's no way to test for upload failures.  And finally, it ties development into the deployment loop: a test won't succeed until the change being tested is running on the TC deployment where CI runs.  That ends up adding weeks to development cycles, as we saw with the client upload/download functionality.  And, it's worth noting, that's not even completely tested, as writing those tests is hard.

In short, I don't think that tests which run against both an upstream Taskcluster deployment and a mock are practical.  Given more engineering bandwidth, maybe we could do some interesting things (perhaps spin up a deployment with minikube for every PR?) but those ideas are immensely far outside our capacity right now.

I appreciate the introduction of a mock, and the use of interfaces to ensure at least some amount of fidelity to the real thing.  But even that is difficult -- I spent a few hours getting the mocks/tests in this PR to work.

Matt and I have had a few conversations about this and we're not sure what the best approach is: how do we handle `kwargs`?

One of the nice things about taking in arbitrary keyword arguments is that canvasapi can maintain parity with Canvas updates as long as the endpoints themselves don't change. The parameters might change types or names or expect new values, but there's nothing we have to do downsteam.

Of course, the downside to that is that there's no useful information for developers in the function signature about what Canvas might accept. Typehints partially address this, but they reintroduce the API parity problem. 

Unless we can come up with a good tradeoff, I wonder if our `kwargs` typehint needs to be `Any` or something equally permissive.

If and when we add multiple issue template, e.g. bug, feature request, question, etc (as has been the plan for at least the main Spyder repo for years now), the original link will allow users to choose them, while the one you suggested will bypass them (as I confirmed in my testing) and not use our issue template at all, while if we don't have them, the two links work equivalently. This is also the canonical link pointed to by the `New Issue` button. Therefore, to keep things as consistent as practical between projects and reduce the risk of future breakage, both of which impact long-term maintainability, I believe we should keep this as-is.

Yeah, on the surface it may seem that way, but on closer investigation its actually quite different.

What we had before was an internal only (`_version.py`) Python module that both `__init__.py` could `import` and `setup.py` could `exec`.

The VERSION file I was referring to is a _plain text file_ (as typically used by third-party version management tools) with the version as a string, which `setup.cfg` would read in with `file:`, but for `__init__.py` to read it, we'd need to correctly include it in `package_data` and/or `MANIFEST.in` (with `include_package_data=True`) to ensure it was included in both sdists and wheels (or the package would crash on import), _and_ then open, read and parse it in `__init__,py` using `importlib.resources`/`importlib_resources`, which itself would either require unnecessarily dropping Python 3.6 support, or adding another third party dependency, increasing further the complexity and cost.

Given that we can now just include the `__version__` string right in `__init__.py` and `setup.cfg` based setuptools can simply read it directly from there with `attr:qtpy.__version__` without executing the file, without adding any of the complex, potentially risky gymnastics that the `file:` approach would require, this clearly seemed to be the simplest and most comparable working alternative.
